title: Bladder Cancer: Diagnosis and Management
objectives: Recognize the typical presentation of bladder cancer
Understand staging and the NMIBC vs MIBC distinction

sections.title: Epidemiology & Risk Factors
sections.content:
**4th most common cancer in men**

**Risk Factors:**
• Smoking (strongest - 50% attributable risk)
• Occupational exposures (aromatic amines, dyes)
• Chronic irritation (catheters, schistosomiasis)
• Cyclophosphamide, radiation
• Age, male sex (3:1)

**Histology:**
• Urothelial (transitional cell): 90%
• Squamous cell: 5%
• Adenocarcinoma: 2%
• Small cell: <1%

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Classic Presentation:**
• Painless gross hematuria (85%)
• Often intermittent
• May have irritative symptoms

**Red Flags for Workup:**
• Age >40 with hematuria
• Smoking history
• Occupational exposure
• Recurrent UTI symptoms without infection

**Workup:**
• Cystoscopy (gold standard)
• CT urogram
• Urine cytology (high-grade sensitivity)

sections.title: Staging
sections.content:
**Critical Distinction: NMIBC vs MIBC**

**Non-Muscle Invasive (NMIBC):**
• Ta: Papillary, confined to mucosa
• Tis: Carcinoma in situ (flat, high-grade)
• T1: Invades lamina propria
• 75% of bladder cancers at presentation

**Muscle Invasive (MIBC):**
• T2: Invades muscularis propria
• T3: Invades perivesical tissue
• T4: Invades adjacent organs

**Key Concept:** T1 high-grade and CIS have high progression risk despite being 'non-muscle invasive'

sections.title: Treatment - NMIBC
sections.content:
**Transurethral Resection (TURBT):**
• Diagnostic AND therapeutic
• Must include muscle in specimen
• Re-TURBT in 4-6 weeks for high-grade T1

**Intravesical Therapy:**
• BCG: Standard for high-risk NMIBC
  - Live attenuated TB vaccine
  - Induction + maintenance
  - Side effects: Cystitis, BCGosis
• Chemotherapy (mitomycin, gemcitabine)
  - For low/intermediate risk
  - Single post-TURBT instillation reduces recurrence

sections.title: Treatment - MIBC
sections.content:
**Radical Cystectomy:**
• Standard of care for MIBC
• Includes pelvic lymphadenectomy
• Urinary diversion (ileal conduit, neobladder)

**Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy:**
• Cisplatin-based (MVAC, GC)
• Improves survival
• Standard before cystectomy

**Bladder Preservation:**
• Trimodal therapy: TURBT + chemo + radiation
• For select patients
• Requires close surveillance

sections.title: Surveillance
sections.content:
**NMIBC Follow-up:**
• Cystoscopy every 3 months x 2 years
• Then every 6 months x 2 years
• Then annually
• Upper tract imaging periodically

**Post-Cystectomy:**
• CT chest/abdomen/pelvis
• Labs, physical exam
• Frequency based on stage/risk

**Recurrence:**
• NMIBC: 50-70% recurrence, 10-20% progression
• High-risk NMIBC: Consider early cystectomy

keyTakeaways: Painless hematuria in smoker = bladder cancer until proven otherwise
75% of bladder cancers are non-muscle invasive at diagnosis
BCG is standard intravesical therapy for high-risk NMIBC
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy improves survival in MIBC
Lifelong surveillance is required due to high recurrence rates

references.citation: AUA Guideline: Non-Muscle Invasive Bladder Cancer

reviewQuestions.question: A 68-year-old smoker presents with painless gross hematuria. Cystoscopy reveals a papillary tumor. TURBT pathology shows high-grade urothelial carcinoma invading lamina propria with muscularis propria present and uninvolved. What is the stage?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Invasion of lamina propria but not muscularis propria is T1. The presence of uninvolved muscle confirms adequate sampling and rules out T2.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected bladder cancer?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in bladder cancer. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of bladder cancer. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing bladder cancer, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
